Center: Center of Nuclear Physics Data, Russian Federal Nuclear Center (VNIIEF), Sarov, Russia
ref: Jour: Nuclear Physics, Section A, Vol.122, p.529 (1968)
title: A study of some 3He induced reactions on 13C and the structure of 16O

x4freetext: A study of some 3He induced reactions on 13C and the
structure of 16O

x4codes.hlp: Duke University, Durham, NC, United States of America
North Carolina State University, Raleigh, NC, United States of America

x4codes.shortRef: Jour: Nuclear Physics, Section A, Vol.122, p.529 (1968)

x4codes.hlp: Duke University, Durham, NC, United States of America

x4freetext: 4 MeV Van de Graaff accelerator.
The doubly ionized 3He beam was used which provided
0.2 mu-A of 3He particles on target with energies up
to 8.2 MeV.
Correction was made in the AUTHOR record.
Corrections were done in Sub 007, 008.
SD: Corrections in all Subents.
The technique of cracking 60%
enriched 13C methyl iodide on a thin nickel foil and
dissolving the foil in acid was used to obtain
self-supporting targets of about 75 ug/cm2 thickness.
Four 300 um surface barrier detectors
mounted in a 30 cm scattering chamber were used.
Carbon build-up on the target introduced an uncertainty
in the percentage of 13C in the target and resulted in
an over-all error of approximately 50%
Statistical error

The technique of cracking 60%
enriched 13C methyl iodide on a thin nickel foil and
dissolving the foil in acid was used to obtain
self-supporting targets of about 75 ug/cm2 thickness.
2 mm lithium-drifted silicon detectors were used
Carbon build-up on the target introduced an uncertainty
in the percentage of 13C in the target and resulted in
an over-all error of approximately 50%

reacs.QuantHlp: Differential c/s with respect to angle

x4freetext: No information about units.
The technique of cracking 60%
enriched 13C methyl iodide on a thin nickel foil and
dissolving the foil in acid was used to obtain
self-supporting targets of about 75 ug/cm2 thickness.
A mass telescope composed of a
proportional counter and a surface barrier detector
was used.
Carbon build-up on the target introduced an uncertainty
in the percentage of 13C in the target and resulted in
an over-all error of approximately 50%

x4freetext: Natural 12C target
A mass telescope composed of a
proportional counter and a surface barrier detector
was used.
An error of less than 20% is estimated for the 12C
absolute cross section measurement
